articles.title: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer Has Resigned. His Replacement Will Likely Be More of the Same.
articles.content:
在任职不到两年后，英国首相基尔·斯塔默已宣布辞职。“我的政党现在问的问题是，我是否是带领我们参加下次大选的最好人选。我听到了议会党团对这个问题的回答，我欣然接受这一回答，”他今天上午在新闻发布会上表示。“我做出的每一个决定都是为了将我热爱的国家放在首位。这就是为什么我将辞去工党领袖职务。”

斯塔默表示，他已与国王通话告知这一决定，并将要求工党管理机构——全国执行委员会制定领导人竞选时间表。提名将于7月9日开始，如果有竞选，继任者将在夏季休会前选出。基尔·斯塔默将留任首相至领导权竞争结束。

这一声明发布前，超过六位内阁部长私下劝他离开唐宁街10号，以及一个周末的猜测——他与妻子在契克斯乡村别墅商讨这一决定。周四，安迪·伯纳姆——大曼彻斯特前市长、取代斯塔默的热门人选——当选为马基菲尔德选区国会议员。被誉为“北方之王”的伯纳姆在该选区获得55%的选票，并在胜选演讲中表示，选民发出了“变革的号召”。根据IPSOS的一项民意调查，他是公众取代斯塔默的首选。

斯塔默的辞职并不特别令人意外。过去几个月对英国政府来说堪称动荡不安，但斯塔默本人从未在普通民众中获得压倒性的支持。根据YouGov的民调，工党在2024年当选执政的主要原因并非其吸引力，而是“为了摆脱保守党”。自担任首相以来，斯塔默目睹了英国经济前景不断恶化：青年失业率飙升，今年第一季度，16至24岁既未就业、未接受教育也未接受培训的“尼特族”比例上升至13.5%——超过100万年轻人。25%的劳动年龄人口处于失业状态，而有工作的人将面临英国历史上最高的税负。如果英国以第51个州的身份加入美国，它将成为最贫穷的州。

随着经济下滑，斯塔默政府卷入了丑闻。彼得·曼德尔森被任命为美国大使以及他与杰弗里·爱泼斯坦的友谊是其中的关键。2024年，当工党捐赠者瓦希德·阿里勋爵获得唐宁街通行证时，现金换通道的质疑开始浮现，报道称斯塔默收到的礼物比任何其他国会议员都多。对移民问题的担忧助长了改革英国党的受欢迎程度。根据Ipsos的民调，移民仍是英国人最重要的问题（尽管净移民人数急剧下降）。

这些事件加上公众对工党移民政策的不满，导致奈杰尔·法拉奇领导的改革英国党在5月地方选举中大获全胜。法拉奇的政党在英格兰赢得1455个地方议会席位，在苏格兰议会赢得17个席位，在威尔士议会赢得34个席位。工党遭遇了有史以来最糟糕的地方选举结果。随后，多位内阁部长相继辞职，包括卫生大臣韦斯·斯特里廷和儿童保护大臣杰斯·菲利普斯。近100名工党议员在5月要求斯塔默辞职。

任何希望成为工党领袖——并因此成为首相——的候选人，都需要获得执政党至少20%的议员支持才能进入竞选。之后，最终决定将通过一人一票制做出，工党成员、附属工会支持者和注册支持者享有同等投票权。几位工党政治家觊觎首相之位，他们都可能让国家规模进一步扩大。

斯塔默的可能继任者伯纳姆因倡导“商业友好型社会主义”和将“人们依赖的基本事物”国有化而登上头条。报道称，伯纳姆经济思想的关键影响者之一是新经济基金会（NEF）前首席执行官米娅塔·法恩布勒。《新政治家》最近的一篇人物简介将“法恩布勒主义”描述为一种植根于合作运动的哲学，认为市场在缺乏更大国家指导的情况下无法公平地实现繁荣。在她执掌NEF期间，该组织提出了最低收入保障、对财富和投资收入征收更高税款、加强消费信贷管控、扩大公共所有权和国家支持投资的作用。最近，法恩布勒赞扬了与伯纳姆新兴的“曼彻斯特主义”议程相关的提案，该议程主张加强对住房、能源、水利和交通的公共控制。

但伯纳姆并非唯一觊觎唐宁街钥匙的工党政治家。前卫生大臣韦斯·斯特里廷已在5月的新闻发布会上宣布参选党魁。他主张“一场有最佳候选人参与的真正竞争”，并表示自己将参选。斯特里廷在2024年7月大选次日登上头条，他承认英国的社会化医疗体系——国家医疗服务体系（NHS）已经“崩溃”，并承诺进行激进的“改革”。然而，他担任卫生大臣期间的记录与前任如出一辙：花费更多纳税人的钱。

前副首相安吉拉·雷纳也是潜在的竞争者。她帮助出台了一些英国最严格的劳动力市场法规，人们经常看到她在夜总会或手里拿着电子烟（尽管她最近已戒掉）。雷纳因税务问题被迫从政府辞职，后来被重新任命为低级部长职位。能源大臣埃德·米利班德——一直是净零排放法规和北海水力压裂禁令的倡导者——在工党内部的人气不断上升，可能也会参选。这并非他第一次——米利班德曾领导工党参加2015年大选，并被戴维·卡梅伦的保守党彻底击败。

随着人气不断上升的绿党施加压力，无论谁领导工党都将被推向左翼。对于希望基尔·斯塔默的辞职可能标志着一个转向——远离高税收、更严格监管和更大国家——的选民来说，可能的继任者阵容几乎没有提供乐观的理由。

《英国首相基尔·斯塔默辞职。其继任者可能延续相同政策。》首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
After less than two years in office, British Prime Minister Keir Starmer has resigned. "The question my party is asking now is whether I am best placed to lead us into the next general election. I have heard the answer of my parliamentary party to that question, and I accept that answer with good grace," he said at a press conference this morning. "Every decision I've taken has been about putting the country I love first. That is why I will resign as leader of the Labour Party."

Starmer said he had spoken to the King to inform him of the decision, and would ask the National Executive Committee, the Labour Party's governing body, to set out a timetable for the leadership race to decide his successor. Nominations will open on July 9, and if there is a contest, his successor will be chosen by the summer recess. Keir Starmer will remain as prime minister until the leadership contest is complete.

The announcement follows more than half a dozen cabinet ministers privately telling him to leave No. 10 Downing Street, and a weekend of speculation that he was mulling over the decision with his wife at the Chequers country retreat. On Thursday, Andy Burnham, the former mayor of Greater Manchester and favourite to replace Starmer, was elected as the member of Parliament for Makerfield. Burnham, nicknamed the "King of the North," won 55 percent of the vote in the constituency and said that voters had issued a "call for change" in his victory speech. He is the public's preferred choice to replace Starmer, according to an IPSOS poll.

Starmer's resignation is not especially surprising. The last few months have been nothing short of tumultuous for the British government, but Starmer himself has never been overwhelmingly popular with the general public. The main reason the Labour Party were elected to power in 2024 was not because of their appeal, but "to get rid of the Conservatives," according to YouGov polling. Since becoming prime minister, Starmer has overseen an ever-worsening economic outlook in the U.K.: Youth unemployment is soaring, with the number of young people aged 16–24 who are NEETs (Not in employment, education or training) increasing to 13.5 percent—more than one million young people—in the first quarter of this year. 25 percent of working-age people are out of work, and those who do have a job are set to pay the highest tax burden in British history. If Britain joined the United States as the 51st state, it would be the poorest state.

As the economy tanked, the Starmer administration was embroiled in scandal. Peter Mandelson's appointment to the role of U.S. Ambassador and his friendship with Jeffrey Epstein were key. Perceptions of cash for access emerged in 2024 when Labour Party donor Lord Waheed Ali was issued a pass to Downing Street, and reports emerged that Starmer had received more gifts than any other member of Parliament. Concerns about immigration fuelled the popularity of the Reform U.K. party. Immigration is still the most important issue for Brits (despite net migration falling sharply), according to an Ipsos poll.

These incidents, coupled with the public's disapproval of Labour's immigration policies, led to a wave of wins for Nigel Farage's Reform U.K. in May's local elections. Farage's party grabbed 1,455 council seats across England, 17 seats in the Scottish Parliament, and 34 seats in the Welsh Parliament. The Labour Party suffered the worst local election result on record.

A series of resignations by Cabinet ministers followed, including Health Secretary Wes Streeting and Safeguarding Minister Jess Philips. Close to 100 Labour members of Parliament called for Starmer's resignation in May.

Any candidate hoping to become leader of the Labour Party, and by consequence, prime minister, will need the support of at least 20 percent of the ruling party's lawmakers to enter the contest. From there, a final decision will be made via a one-member-one-vote system in which Labour Party members, affiliated trade union supporters, and registered supporters all vote equally. There are a few Labour Party politicians with their eyes on the role of prime minister, all of whom are likely to make the size of the state even bigger.

Burnham, Starmer's likely successor, has made headlines for promoting "business-friendly socialism" and the nationalization of "basic things that people depend on." Reports suggest that one of the key influences on Burnham's economic thinking is Miatta Fahnbulleh, the former chief executive of the New Economics Foundation (NEF). A recent New Statesman profile described "Fahnbullehism" as a philosophy rooted in the co-operative movement that sees markets as incapable of delivering prosperity fairly without much greater state direction. During her time at NEF, the organization proposed a minimum income guarantee, higher taxes on wealth and investment income, tighter controls on consumer credit, and a larger role for public ownership and state-backed investment. More recently, Fahnbulleh has praised proposals associated with Burnham's emerging "Manchesterism" agenda, which argues for greater public control over housing, energy, water, and transport.

But Burnham is not the only Labour Party politician with his eyes on the keys to No. 10. Wes Streeting, the former health secretary, already launched his leadership bid at a press conference in May. He argued for "a proper contest with the best candidates on the field", and said that he would be standing. Streeting made headlines the day after the July 2024 general election for admitting that the U.K.'s socialized healthcare system, the National Health Service, was "broken" and promising radical "reform." However, his track record as health secretary has been much more of the same strategy his predecessors opted for: spending more taxpayer cash.

Angela Rayner, the former deputy prime minister, is also a potential contender. She helped introduce some of Britain's most stringent labor market regulations, and is often seen at a nightclub or with a vape in her hand (although she has recently quit). Rayner was forced to resign from the government over her tax affairs, and was later reappointed to a junior ministerial position.

Ed Miliband, the energy secretary who has championed net zero regulations and a ban on fracking in the North Sea, has increased his popularity within the Labour Party and could throw his hat in the ring. This wouldn't be the first time—Miliband led the Labour Party into the 2015 general election, and received a crushing defeat at the hands of David Cameron's Conservative Party.

With pressure mounting from the increasingly popular Green Party, whoever leads the Labour Party next will be pulled to the left. For voters hoping Keir Starmer's resignation might mark a turn away from higher taxes, heavier regulation, and a larger state, the likely field of successors offers little reason for optimism.

The post British Prime Minister Keir Starmer Has Resigned. His Replacement Will Likely Be More of the Same. appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Brickbat: Taking a Bite
articles.content: 大巴克卡真厨房的业主表示，佐治亚州大学城的官员不公平地针对他们的餐厅，进行反复检查、发出罚单，并指控他们非法经营夜总会，尽管他们声称拥有适当的许可证照。餐厅老板肖恩·珀金斯和她的商业伙伴认为，这些行动是由副市长乔·卡恩推动的，市律师否认了这一说法。然而，大学城市长比安卡·莫特利·布鲁姆公开支持珀金斯，称市政府的行动是骚扰，并表示民选官员不应利用权力针对企业。卡恩没有回应当地电视台的置评请求。
articles.summary:
The owners of Big Back's Cajun Kitchen say the officials in College Park, Georgia, have unfairly targeted their restaurant with repeated inspections, citations, and accusations that it is operating an illegal nightclub, even though they say they have the proper permits and licenses. Restaurant owner Shawn Perkins and her business partners believe the actions were prompted by Mayor Pro Tem Joe Carn, a claim the city attorney denies. However, College Park Mayor Bianca Motley Broom publicly supported Perkins, calling the city's actions harassment and saying elected officials should not use their power to target businesses. Carn did not respond to requests from a local TV station for comment.

The post Brickbat: Taking a Bite appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Brazil Moves To End the Six-Day Workweek
articles.content:
约三分之一的巴西正规就业人员实行“6x1”工作周——工作六天休息一天——这种情况在航空、酒店、医疗、零售和餐饮服务等行业尤为普遍。五月下旬，巴西众议院通过了一项宪法修正案，将有效禁止这种工作安排，并将该提案提交参议院批准。

该提案将把巴西宪法规定的每周工作时长上限从44小时降至40小时，并要求每周提供两天带薪休息日。在巴西，服务行业工作者通常获得固定月薪，而非像美国那样更常见的按小时计薪。由于该修正案将禁止雇主降低这些薪资以反映缩短的工作时间，雇主将不得不在大约减少10%的工作时间内支付相同的月薪。

该修正案由联邦众议员埃里卡·希尔顿提出，她是巴西下议院社会党与自由党的成员。在希尔顿看来，“每周工作六天只为了休息一天不是生活，这是剥削……你不能只活在自己生命的七分之一中。”

该提案迅速引发巴西民众关注，并在政治上获得动力，在第二轮投票中以461票对19票通过下议院。对于演员和社交媒体网红来说，公开支持这项措施似乎几乎是强制性的。在一段观看量超过100万次的Instagram短视频中，女演员莱蒂西亚·科林宣称：“6x1是一个政治项目。这是一个旨在让工人筋疲力尽的体系。”最近的一项民调发现，63%的巴西人支持结束6x1工作安排。

支持者认为，这项改革对于服务业中的辛苦工作者来说早该实施。“我知道站了8小时、10小时、12小时后脚肿是什么感觉。我之所以知道，是因为我亲身经历过，”来自总统路易斯·伊纳西奥·卢拉·达席尔瓦所在工人党的联邦众议员丹达拉·托南辛说道。卢拉正在寻求今年连任。

但禁止6x1更可能伤害那些本应得到帮助的工人。该提案迫使雇主为更少的工作时间支付相同薪资，将提高正规劳动的时薪成本，却不增加工人的生产率。为了应对更高的劳动力成本，企业可能会减少招聘、提高价格或尽可能实现自动化。其结果将是把更多员工推向非正规部门，而该部门已占巴西约40%的劳动力。事实上，许多6x1工作者已经在非正规部门工作，因此根本不会受到该修正案的影响。

正如少数投票反对该修正案的联邦众议员之一金·卡塔吉里在其演讲中所解释的那样，几乎每个人都希望看到6x1工作安排的结束——分歧在于该修正案是否真的能实现这一目标。“我不会欺骗工人，告诉他仅仅因为宪法现在规定他的工作安排将是5x2，这就会在实践中实现，”卡塔吉里说。“那是一个谎言……它越早开始，人们就越早意识到这是一场闹剧；他们的生活没有改变，没有改善。”

该提案还可能使入门级服务工作更难找到。年轻且缺乏经验的工人在变得有生产力之前需要更多的培训和时间。如果雇用人员的时薪成本上升，雇主可能更有动力偏向有经验的工人而非初次求职者。即使对于那些保住工作的人来说，禁止6x1也可能使服务工作变得更加不愉快。许多这类工作依赖于每班次的多名工人。根据新规定，雇主可能只是期望现有员工在更少的同事在岗情况下处理相同的客流量。

更普遍地说，该提案基于一个错误的前提，即每个人都希望减少工作时间。事实证明，大多数人不想减少工作时间；他们想赚更多钱。这与约翰·梅纳德·凯恩斯1930年论文《我们孙辈的经济可能性》背后的错误假设相同，他在文中推断，如果他在有生之年观察到的生产率增长持续下去，他的孙辈每周只需工作15小时——而且主要是为了娱乐。然而，100多年后，美国全职工作的普通人每周至少工作40小时。这是因为我们中的许多人在更短的每周工作时间和大房子、更好的医疗保健、假期和教育之间选择了后者。

这种权衡在巴西更为尖锐，因为宪法修正案主要影响该国一些收入最低的人。他们中许多人的工资接近最低工资，约为每月300美元。对于基本需求仍未满足的工人来说，多一天的休息可能不会感觉像解放；它可能只是成为从事副业的时间。他们不快的根源不是缺乏自由时间，而是贫困。

巴西六天工作周的普遍存在是经济停滞的症状。更短的工作周和更好的工作条件源于生产率提高和劳动力竞争。劳动法只是将这些成果编纂成文。

全面禁止苛刻的工作安排并非解决方案。巴西工人需要生产率增长、更多的正规就业机会，以及一个雇主相互竞争他们的劳动力市场。在生产率提高实现之前强制推行全面改善工作条件，只会排斥最脆弱的工人——延缓他们的经济上升。

本文首次发布于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
About one-third of Brazilians in formal employment have a "6x1" workweek—six days of work followed by one day of rest—which is particularly common in sectors such as air travel, hotels, healthcare, retail, and food service. In late May, Brazil's Chamber of Deputies approved a constitutional amendment that would effectively ban this work arrangement, sending the proposal to the Senate for ratification. 

The proposal would reduce Brazil's constitutionally set cap on weekly working hours from 44 to 40 and require two paid rest days per week. In Brazil, service workers are typically paid a fixed monthly salary rather than an hourly wage, as is more common in the United States. Because the amendment would prohibit employers from reducing those salaries to reflect the shorter schedule, employers would have to pay the same monthly wage for roughly 10 percent fewer hours of work. 

The amendment was introduced by federal deputy Erika Hilton, a member of Brazil's lower house from the Socialism and Liberty Party (PSOL). In Hilton's view, "Working six days just to get one day off isn't a life. It's exploitation….You can't live only one-seventh of your own life."

The proposal quickly captured Brazilian attention and gained political momentum, clearing the lower house in a 461–19 second-round vote. For actors and social media influencers, public support for the measure seemed almost mandatory. In an Instagram Reel with over 1 million views, actress Letícia Colin declared: "6x1 is a political project. It's a system created to keep workers exhausted." A recent poll found that 63 percent of Brazilians support ending the 6x1 work schedule.

Supporters argue this is a long-overdue reform for workers in grueling service-sector jobs. "I know what it feels like to have swollen feet from standing for eight, 10, 12 hours. I know because I lived it," said Dandara Tonantzin, a federal deputy from the Workers' Party, the party of President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, who is seeking reelection this year.

But banning 6x1 is more likely to harm the very workers it is supposed to help. By forcing employers to pay the same salary for fewer hours of work, the proposal would raise the hourly cost of formal labor without increasing workers' productivity. To manage higher labor costs, businesses may hire fewer workers, raise prices, or automate where possible.

The result would be to push more employees to the informal sector, which already accounts for about 40 percent of workers in Brazil. In fact, many 6x1 workers are already in the informal sector and thus would not be affected by the amendment at all.

As Kim Kataguiri, one of the few federal deputies who voted against the amendment, explained in his floor speech, almost everyone would like to see the end of the 6x1 schedule—the disagreement is over whether this amendment will actually deliver it. "I am not going to lie to a worker and tell him that just because the constitution now says his schedule will be 5x2, that will happen in practice," Katarguiri said. "That is a lie….The sooner it starts, the sooner people will realize it's a farce; that their lives haven't changed, haven't improved."

The proposal would also likely make entry-level service work harder to find. Young and inexperienced workers require more training and time on the clock before they become productive. If the hourly cost of employing someone rises, employers will likely have stronger incentives to favor experienced workers over first-time job seekers.

Even for those who keep their jobs, banning 6x1 might make service jobs even more unpleasant. Many of these jobs depend on multiple workers per shift. Under the new rule, employers might simply expect the current workforce to handle the same customer flow with fewer coworkers on the floor.

More generally, the proposal rests on the faulty premise that everyone wants to work less. As it turns out, most people don't want to work less; they want to earn more.

It's the same faulty assumption underpinning John Maynard Keynes' 1930 essay "Economic Possibilities for Our Grandchildren," in which he theorized that, if the productivity gains he observed in his lifetime continued, his grandchildren would work only 15 hours a week—and mostly for fun.

Yet, more than 100 years later, the average American with a full-time job works at least 40 hours a week. That's because many of us have chosen larger homes, better health care, vacations, and education over shorter workweeks.

The tradeoff is even sharper in Brazil because the constitutional amendment would primarily affect some of the country's lowest earners. Many of them earn salaries near the minimum wage, which is roughly $300 a month. For workers with basic needs still unmet, an extra day of rest may not feel like liberation; it may simply become time for a side gig. The source of their discomfort is not the lack of free time. It's poverty.

The prevalence of the six-day workweek in Brazil is a symptom of a stagnant economy. Shorter workweeks and better working conditions emerge from productivity gains and competition for labor. Labor law merely codifies these gains.

An outright ban on demanding work schedules is not the solution. Brazilian workers need productivity growth, more formal job opportunities, and a labor market in which employers compete for them. Mandating across-the-board improvements in working conditions before productivity gains materialize will only exclude the most vulnerable workers—delaying their economic ascent.

The post Brazil Moves To End the Six-Day Workweek appeared first on Reason.com.
